ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Japanese nabe salmon set meal. FOODON_00002809 (set meal food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish and fish products — composite dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (fish-based composite dish) is the closest defensible code for a multi-component Japanese set meal centred on salmon. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for nabe salmon teishoku.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Japanese nabe salmon set meal. SNOMED CT coverage of composite ethnic set meals is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard set meal serving of approximately 650 g, comprising salmon nabe hot pot, steamed or sushi rice, miso soup, pickled vegetables, and tempura components.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Salmon, Atlantic, farmed, cooked; Rice, white, cooked; Tempura, seafood; Miso soup; composite estimation
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for a composite Japanese nabe salmon set meal. Nutrient values were estimated by component-weighted averaging using USDA FoodData Central entries for Atlantic salmon (cooked, farmed), white rice (cooked), seafood tempura, miso soup, tofu, and mixed vegetables. The input record's per-100g values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against this USDA component estimation and found to be broadly consistent. Values are retained with medium confidence. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients are set to -1 where USDA component-level data for the exact composite preparation is not reliably disaggregated.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — White rice, cooked (analogous anchor)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a composite Japanese nabe salmon set meal. GI estimated at 60 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate component being white rice (GI ~64–72 per University of Sydney GI Database) moderated by substantial protein from salmon (10 g/100g), dietary fibre (1.23 g/100g), and fat (6.92 g/100g), which collectively lower the effective postprandial glycaemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (60 × 85.0) / 100 = 51.0, where carbohydrate per 650 g serving = 13.08 × 6.5 = 85.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 338 mg/100g; estimated 2200 mg per full 650 g serving from soy sauce, miso soup, dashi broth, and pickled vegetables
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and miso soup, limit pickled vegetable portions, and be aware that a full serving may contribute approximately 95–110% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (13.1 g/100g, ~85 g per serving) from white rice and tempura batter; estimated glycemic load of 51 per serving is high
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should monitor portion size carefully, preferring a half-serving of rice, requesting minimal tempura, and leveraging the high salmon protein (10 g/100g) and omega-3 content to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Salmon provides cardioprotective EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids; however, sodium at 338 mg/100g and deep-fried tempura components elevate cardiovascular risk if consumed in full portions regularly
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease may benefit from the salmon omega-3 content but should limit the tempura component to reduce saturated fat and sodium intake, and request reduced soy sauce and miso soup to keep the full-serving sodium below 1500 mg.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Salmon EPA/DHA omega-3 fatty acids are strongly anti-inflammatory and recommended by EULAR; tempura AGEs and high sodium from condiments are pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should maximise the salmon nabe component for its EPA/DHA content while limiting deep-fried tempura and requesting reduced soy sauce and miso soup to minimise AGE and sodium-driven inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Nabe Salmon Set Meal (鍋サーモンセット) is a comprehensive Japanese teishoku-style set meal centred on salmon prepared in a nabe (Japanese hot pot) style, typically served in Japanese restaurants in Singapore. The set commonly includes a salmon nabe hot pot with dashi broth, tofu, mushrooms (shiitake, enoki), nori, and seasonal vegetables; steamed white rice or sushi rice; miso soup; pickled vegetables (tsukemono); and may include salmon sashimi, grilled salmon, or seafood tempura as supplementary components. The nabe preparation involves simmering salmon and vegetables in a kombu or dashi-based broth seasoned with soy sauce and mirin, producing a savoury, umami-rich dish with tender fish and soft vegetables. The tempura component, when present, adds a crispy deep-fried texture contrast. Per 100 g, the set provides approximately 146 kcal, 10 g protein, 6.9 g fat, and 13.1 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 338 mg primarily from soy sauce, miso, and dashi. Salmon is one of the richest dietary sources of EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids, supporting cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory health. The set is moderate in glycaemic load due to white rice content and is suitable for most adults in controlled portions. Clinically, sodium management is the primary concern for hypertensive individuals, while the glycaemic load from rice requires monitoring for type 2 diabetes patients. The dish is not halal-certified in most Singapore outlets and contains fish, shellfish, soy, and wheat allergens.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite Japanese nabe salmon set meal as served in Singapore. Nutrient values were cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central component entries for Atlantic salmon (cooked), white rice (cooked), seafood tempura, miso soup, and tofu. The input record's ai_generated values (confidence 0.7) are broadly consistent with this component-level estimation. Medium confidence is assigned because the exact recipe composition, broth volume, and condiment quantities are not independently verified from an approved primary source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid disaggregation for this composite Japanese set meal is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024 for the exact preparation. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for the composite dish is not reliably available in approved sources for this preparation. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this composite Japanese nabe salmon set meal are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024 for the exact multi-component preparation. Core macros (protein_g 10.0, fat_g 6.92, carbohydrate_g 13.08) are all non-zero and consistent with a mixed Japanese set meal containing salmon, rice, tofu, and vegetables. trans_fat_g 0.03 is a trace value consistent with minor contributions from deep-fried tempura components. GI estimated at 60 (medium) using white rice as the dominant carbohydrate anchor from the University of Sydney GI Database, moderated by protein and fat content. Glycemic load per 650 g serving calculated as 51.0. The dish is not halal-certified in most Singapore Japanese restaurant contexts. Allergens include fish, shellfish, soy, and wheat. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was noted but foodo.sg is not an approved primary nutrient source; USDA FoodData Central was used as the approved source for nutrient estimation.
